pairs.definition_drop: Whether the household's expressed lifestyle and values lean toward mutual interdependence and communal engagement with neighbours, or toward friendly-but-independent private coexistence.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the application essays are grounded in a genuine personal voice, concrete verifiable experiences, named organisations, and specific past outcomes — as opposed to generic language, corporate jargon, or terse non-answers that leave the committee unable to assess fit.
pairs.reason: A highly communal applicant who writes in vague, jargon-laden prose scores high on solidarity disposition but low on essay specificity, while a private-leaning applicant who writes with precise, named examples scores the reverse.

pairs.definition_drop: Whether the household's expressed lifestyle and values lean toward mutual interdependence and communal engagement with neighbours, or toward friendly-but-independent private coexistence.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant's stated motivation for seeking co-op housing centres on cooperative values — mutual aid, shared responsibility, community-building — as ends in themselves, as opposed to primarily instrumental goals such as rent affordability or housing stability.
pairs.reason: An applicant can be deeply motivated by co-op values (mutual aid, shared governance) as an ideological commitment yet still prefer low-key, arm's-length neighbour relations in daily life — high on intrinsic motivation, low on communal interdependence.

pairs.definition_drop: The degree to which the application essays are grounded in a genuine personal voice, concrete verifiable experiences, named organisations, and specific past outcomes — as opposed to generic language, corporate jargon, or terse non-answers that leave the committee unable to assess fit.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant's stated motivation for seeking co-op housing centres on cooperative values — mutual aid, shared responsibility, community-building — as ends in themselves, as opposed to primarily instrumental goals such as rent affordability or housing stability.
pairs.reason: An applicant can be genuinely motivated by cooperative values but express them in generic slogans with no named organisations or concrete outcomes, scoring high on intrinsic motivation yet low on essay specificity — and vice versa.

pairs.definition_drop: Whether the household's expressed lifestyle and values lean toward mutual interdependence and communal engagement with neighbours, or toward friendly-but-independent private coexistence.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant describes valuing and actively nurturing relationships with neighbours and co-residents — through hospitality, care, and genuine community-building — beyond fulfilling formal obligations.
pairs.reason: Both definitions measure the same underlying axis — the degree to which an applicant actively engages in and values communal neighbouring relationships — and would score any applicant alike for the same reasons.

pairs.definition_drop: Whether the household's expressed lifestyle and values lean toward mutual interdependence and communal engagement with neighbours, or toward friendly-but-independent private coexistence.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant explicitly commits — in their own words — to the full range of co-op participation obligations: work hours, committee roles, AGM attendance, and shared-governance duties, framed as a personal value rather than an obligation to be minimised.
pairs.name_keep: Stated Commitment to Active Co-op Participation
pairs.reason: An applicant can explicitly commit to all formal co-op obligations (work hours, committees, AGM) while still preferring independent private coexistence in daily neighbourly life — high on participation commitment, low on communal interdependence.

pairs.definition_drop: How concrete and actionable the household's described contributions are, versus vague or generic pledges — regardless of what the specific skills are.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the application essays are grounded in a genuine personal voice, concrete verifiable experiences, named organisations, and specific past outcomes — as opposed to generic language, corporate jargon, or terse non-answers that leave the committee unable to assess fit.
pairs.reason: Both definitions measure the same quality — groundedness in concrete, specific, verifiable detail rather than vague generic language — and would score any applicant alike for the same reason regardless of domain.

pairs.definition_drop: How concrete and actionable the household's described contributions are, versus vague or generic pledges — regardless of what the specific skills are.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant's stated motivation for seeking co-op housing centres on cooperative values — mutual aid, shared responsibility, community-building — as ends in themselves, as opposed to primarily instrumental goals such as rent affordability or housing stability.
pairs.reason: An applicant deeply motivated by cooperative values can still describe their contributions in vague, generic terms, scoring high on intrinsic motivation but low on contribution specificity.

pairs.definition_drop: How concrete and actionable the household's described contributions are, versus vague or generic pledges — regardless of what the specific skills are.
pairs.definition_keep: Whether the household's expressed lifestyle and values lean toward mutual interdependence and communal engagement with neighbours, or toward friendly-but-independent private coexistence.
pairs.reason: A communally-oriented applicant can describe their neighbourly values in vague, warm but unactionable language, scoring high on solidarity disposition yet low on contribution specificity.

pairs.definition_drop: How concrete and actionable the household's described contributions are, versus vague or generic pledges — regardless of what the specific skills are.
pairs.definition_keep: The number of distinct, non-overlapping domains — physical trades, administration/coordination, finance, social programming, digital/communications — in which the household offers concrete, named skills, reducing the co-op's dependence on other members across multiple functions.
pairs.reason: An applicant can name one single, narrow but highly concrete skill (high specificity, low breadth) or list many domains in vague terms (high breadth, low specificity), making these genuinely orthogonal axes.

pairs.definition_drop: The degree to which the applicant demonstrates genuine, personal reflection on what cooperative housing means — going beyond slogans to show they have grappled with the tensions, trade-offs, and realities of shared governance.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant's stated motivation for seeking co-op housing centres on cooperative values — mutual aid, shared responsibility, community-building — as ends in themselves, as opposed to primarily instrumental goals such as rent affordability or housing stability.
pairs.name_drop: Depth of Reflection on Co-op Living
pairs.reason: An applicant can be genuinely motivated by co-op values as ends in themselves yet show little reflective depth about governance trade-offs or tensions, while a philosophically reflective applicant might ultimately be motivated primarily by affordability.

pairs.definition_drop: The depth of skills the household brings to co-op financial administration — budgeting, bookkeeping, audit preparation, treasurer duties, minute-taking, election management, and financial oversight.
pairs.definition_keep: Depth of hands-on experience managing or auditing collective finances — bookkeeping, budget preparation, account reconciliation, audit readiness, and treasurer roles in a co-op or comparable shared-governance organisation.
pairs.reason: Both definitions measure the same underlying axis — hands-on capability in co-op financial administration (bookkeeping, budgets, audits, treasurer duties) — and would score any applicant alike for the same reasons.

pairs.definition_drop: The degree to which the applicant describes valuing and actively nurturing relationships with neighbours and co-residents — through hospitality, care, and genuine community-building — beyond fulfilling formal obligations.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant's stated motivation for seeking co-op housing centres on cooperative values — mutual aid, shared responsibility, community-building — as ends in themselves, as opposed to primarily instrumental goals such as rent affordability or housing stability.
pairs.reason: An applicant motivated by abstract cooperative principles (mutual aid, shared governance) may not particularly value warm, hospitality-driven personal relationships with neighbours, scoring high on intrinsic motivation but low on interpersonal warmth.

pairs.definition_drop: The count of dependent children in the household, affecting demand on shared spaces, the social texture of the co-op, and the household's available adult time for co-op obligations.
pairs.definition_keep: The total number of people in the household (adults plus children), relevant to unit occupancy, space needs, and — in larger households — the potential for multiple adults to contribute to co-op work.
pairs.reason: A household of four adults with no children scores high on total size/capacity but zero on dependent children, while a single parent with three children scores the reverse — these are concretely distinct axes.

pairs.definition_drop: The degree to which the applicant demonstrates genuine, personal reflection on what cooperative housing means — going beyond slogans to show they have grappled with the tensions, trade-offs, and realities of shared governance.
pairs.definition_keep: The depth and directness of prior experience living in or holding a governance role within a housing co-op specifically — including first-hand knowledge of work hours, shared decision-making, consensus cycles, and co-op rhythms.
pairs.name_drop: Depth of Reflection on Co-op Living
pairs.reason: A long-time co-op resident with deep practical governance experience may articulate little genuine philosophical reflection on its tensions, while a first-time applicant can demonstrate profound reflective depth despite zero direct experience.

pairs.definition_drop: Whether the household's expressed lifestyle and values lean toward mutual interdependence and communal engagement with neighbours, or toward friendly-but-independent private coexistence.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant demonstrates genuine, personal reflection on what cooperative housing means — going beyond slogans to show they have grappled with the tensions, trade-offs, and realities of shared governance.
pairs.name_keep: Depth of Reflection on Co-op Living
pairs.reason: An applicant can demonstrate deep, nuanced reflection on co-op governance trade-offs while still personally preferring friendly-but-independent coexistence with neighbours — high on reflectiveness, low on communal interdependence.

settled.decision: The kept axis already covers formal governance in any shared-governance body. Report 1's adjacent_governance_experience, Report 2's analogous_governance_experience, and Report 4's governance_and_admin_experience all measure the same thing — governance experience in co-op-adjacent bodies. Report 3's administrative_coordination_skill overlaps substantially (running elections, keeping minutes, scheduling work shifts) with governance administration; the maintenance-coordination component of that axis is absorbed into the separate maintenance_coordination_skill axis. All these re-carvings of governance administration would score the same applicant the same way and are merged into the kept axis.
settled.name: Formal Collective Governance Experience

settled.decision: Kept separate from governance_board_experience because a plausible applicant could have extensive strata-council or childcare-co-op board experience (high on governance_board_experience) but zero housing co-op experience (low here), and vice versa — a lifelong housing co-op resident may never have held a formal governance role in any analogous body. The four source keys across Reports 0–4 all measure this same housing-co-op-specific familiarity and are merged here.
settled.name: Housing Co-op–Specific Experience

settled.decision: All five source keys across Reports 0–4 measure the same underlying competency — treasurer-level or bookkeeping-level financial skill applicable to co-op administration. They would score the same applicant the same way. Merged into one settled axis. Note: the broader governance-admin component of Report 3's financial_governance_skill (budget literacy in governance settings) is captured here, while its knowledge-transfer component is absorbed into knowledge_transfer_orientation.
settled.name: Financial & Bookkeeping Skill

settled.decision: Reports 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 all measure the same concept — licensed or professionally practised trade skill for interior building maintenance. All would score the same applicant identically. Merged. Note: Report 2 uses the key licensed_trade_maintenance_skill and Report 3 uses the same key — both are absorbed here. Arboriculture/outdoor trade skills are deliberately kept separate under grounds_outdoor_skill since a qualified arborist scores high there but may have no interior trade skill.
settled.name: Licensed / Trade Maintenance Skill

settled.decision: Reports 0, 1, and 2 (maintenance_coordination_skill / maintenance_coordination_experience) all measure the same thing — the administrative-oversight side of building maintenance as distinct from doing the physical work. Kept separate from trade_maintenance_skill (doing the work) and from governance_board_experience (governance administration), because a person could be a skilled tradesperson who never coordinates contractors, or an experienced governance administrator who cannot schedule trades. The maintenance-coordination component of Report 3's administrative_coordination_skill is absorbed here.
settled.name: Maintenance Coordination & Contractor Management

settled.decision: All five source keys across Reports 0–4 measure the same concept — specialist outdoor and horticultural skill applicable to shared grounds. Kept separate from trade_maintenance_skill because a qualified arborist or horticulturalist scores high here but may have no interior building trade skill, and an electrician scores high on trade_maintenance_skill but may have no outdoor expertise.
settled.name: Grounds, Gardening & Outdoor Maintenance Skill

settled.decision: All five source keys across Reports 0–4 measure the same concept — the ability to build and maintain co-op-relevant digital infrastructure. All would score the same applicant the same way. Merged into one settled axis.
settled.name: Digital / Tech Infrastructure Skill

settled.decision: Reports 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 all measure the same professional/practised skill in mediating interpersonal disputes. All source keys are merged here. Kept separate from conflict_history_transparency (the kept axis about disclosed personal conflict history and reflection quality) because a professional mediator with strong skills could still have a personal history of board conflicts, and vice versa — these measure different things.
settled.name: Conflict Mediation & People-Management Skill

settled.decision: Reports 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 all measure the same concept — whether a qualified health professional is in the household and offers to support community safety. All would score the same applicant the same way. Merged into one settled axis.
settled.name: Health & Safety Skill

settled.decision: Report 4's community_outreach_and_social_services_skills is kept as a distinct settled axis. A plausible applicant could score high here (professional interpreter connecting neighbours to city services) while scoring low on conflict_mediation_skill (no HR or dispute-resolution training) or community_programming_skill (no event organisation experience). The social-services / language-access dimension is genuinely different from both.
settled.name: Community Outreach & Social Services Skill

settled.decision: Reports 0, 1, 2, 3 community_programming_skill/contribution and Report 4's co_op_cultural_and_social_programming_contribution all measure the same underlying capacity to organise internal community events and programming. Report 4's youth_program_and_family_community_contribution is a subset (youth-focused programming); merging is warranted because a youth soccer coach who runs homework clubs would score high on both — they are not independently scoreable in a way that differentiates the pool. All merged here.
settled.name: Community Programming & Social Infrastructure Skill

settled.decision: Committee-requested kept axis. Reports 0, 3, and 4 use participation_commitment; Reports 1 and 2 use stated_participation_commitment. All five measure the same concept — the applicant's explicit verbal commitment to co-op participation obligations. All merged into the kept axis under its key. The committee request is preserved.
settled.name: Stated Commitment to Active Co-op Participation

settled.decision: Committee-requested kept axis. All five source keys across Reports 0–4 measure the same concept — the household's actual discretionary time for co-op work after external commitments. All merged into the kept axis under its key. The committee request is preserved.
settled.name: Realistic Available Time for Co-op Work

settled.decision: Committee-requested kept axis. All five source keys across Reports 0–4 measure the same concept — whether the applicant's motivation for co-op living is intrinsic (values-based) versus instrumental (cost-based). All merged into the kept axis under its key. The committee request is preserved.
settled.name: Intrinsic Co-op Philosophy Alignment

settled.decision: Kept as a distinct settled axis from coop_motivation_intrinsic. A person could articulate strong cooperative values (high on coop_motivation_intrinsic) while offering only slogans with no evidence of deep personal reflection on tensions and trade-offs (low here). Conversely, a person could show deep, nuanced reflection on co-op challenges without framing their primary motivation as intrinsically values-based. Report 2 surfaces this as a standalone axis; no other report captures it in this form.
settled.name: Depth of Reflection on Co-op Living

settled.decision: Committee-requested kept axis. Reports 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 all use collaborative_disposition; Report 2 also has comfort_with_consensus_process (patience with collective decision-making processes specifically). These would score the same applicant the same way — a person patient with consensus is by definition collaborative in governance, and vice versa. Both are merged into the kept axis under its key. The committee request is preserved.
settled.name: Collaborative Disposition in Shared Governance

settled.decision: Committee-requested kept axis. Kept separate from conflict_mediation_skill (professional/practised skill in resolving others' disputes) and from self_awareness_transparency (broad candid disclosure of any limitation). This axis is specifically about the applicant's own disclosed history of governance conflicts and the quality of their reflection on it. No discovery report produced a key that re-carves exactly this concept — it stands alone. The committee request is preserved.
settled.name: Constructive Conflict Disposition & History

settled.decision: Kept as a distinct settled axis from conflict_history_transparency. The latter is specifically about governance-conflict history and reflection on it; self_awareness_transparency is broader — it covers any candid disclosure of limitations (time constraints, physical limitations, etc.) even where no conflict history exists. A person could disclose a scheduling limitation honestly (high here) while having no governance-conflict history to reflect on (not scoreable on conflict_history_transparency). Report 3 surfaces this as a standalone concept.
settled.name: Self-Awareness & Honest Disclosure

settled.decision: Committee-requested kept axis. All six source keys across Reports 0–4 measure the same concept — whether the household has a named personal connection to a current member of this specific co-op. All merged into the kept axis under its key. The committee request is preserved.
settled.name: Existing Personal Connection to This Co-op

settled.decision: Committee-requested kept axis. Reports 0 (outdoor_community_lifestyle_fit), 1 (outside_community_engagement), 2 (mutual_aid_community_engagement), and 3 (lifestyle_community_orientation) all measure the same concept — the household's existing pattern of community volunteering and civic engagement outside the co-op. All merged into the kept axis under its key. The committee request is preserved.
settled.name: Broader Volunteer & Civic Engagement

settled.decision: Committee-requested kept axis. All six source keys across Reports 0–4 deal with children's ages and their downstream effects on household stability and adult availability. While some source keys emphasise contribution potential and others emphasise supervision burden, all would score the same applicant the same way — a family with teenagers scores high on all of them; a family with toddlers scores low. Merged into the kept axis under its key, with the definition updated to incorporate both the stability-horizon framing (kept axis) and the availability/contribution framing (discovered axes). The committee request is preserved.
settled.name: Children's Age — Household Stability Horizon

settled.decision: Reports 0, 1, 2, and 4 all use long_term_residency_intent; Report 3 uses permanence_intent. All measure the same concept — whether the applicant intends permanent versus transitional membership. Merged into one settled axis. No kept axis covers this concept.
settled.name: Long-Term Residency Intent

settled.decision: Kept as a distinct settled axis from collaborative_governance_disposition. The collaborative disposition axis is specifically about patience within governance processes (meetings, consensus, committees); this axis is about whether the applicant wants communal social life with neighbours at all — independent of governance. A person could be patient and constructive in meetings (high on collaborative_governance_disposition) while still preferring to keep to themselves outside of obligatory governance contexts (low here). Report 3 surfaces this as a standalone concept with a genuinely different pole.
settled.name: Peer-Solidarity vs. Private-Coexistence Disposition

settled.decision: Reports 0, 1, 2, and 3 all measure the same meta-quality — how concretely the applicant describes their contributions, irrespective of what those contributions are. All would score the same applicant the same way. Merged into one settled axis.
settled.name: Specificity of Contribution Offer

settled.decision: Reports 0, 2, 3, and 4 all measure the same concept — the habit of documenting processes and mentoring successors to reduce institutional fragility. All would score the same applicant the same way. Merged into one settled axis.
settled.name: Knowledge Transfer & Succession Orientation

settled.decision: Reports 0 (new_member_integration_skill) and 2 (orientation_and_onboarding_skill) measure the same concept — experience running new-member onboarding and integration. Kept separate from community_programming_skill (which covers ongoing social events) and from governance_board_experience (which covers formal governance roles), because a person could be experienced at welcoming and integrating new members without being a governance administrator or event organiser.
settled.name: New Member Integration & Onboarding Skill

settled.decision: Reports 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 all measure the same concept — how long the household has been at its current income level, assessed via employment tenure. All would score the same applicant the same way. Merged into one settled axis. Kept separate from income_concentration_risk (which is about distribution between earners) and from financial_headroom (which is about absolute income level).
settled.name: Employment Tenure Stability

settled.decision: Reports 0 (income_resilience), 1 (income_concentration_risk + income_share_balance), 2 (income_concentration_risk), 3 (income_concentration_risk), and 4 (dual_income_resilience) all measure the same concept — the symmetry of income distribution between two earners as a measure of resilience. Report 1 split this into two keys (income_concentration_risk and income_share_balance) but these are two framings of the same underlying metric; they would score the same applicant identically. All merged into one settled axis.
settled.name: Dual-Income Resilience (Earnings Balance)

settled.decision: Reports 1, 2, 3, and 4 all measure the same concept — absolute income level relative to housing costs as a payment-reliability buffer. All would score the same applicant the same way. Merged into one settled axis. Kept separate from income_to_coop_range_fit (which measures the upper-bound concern — whether income is so high that the co-op's pricing is no longer a meaningful draw, affecting retention) and from income_concentration_risk (which measures distribution, not level).
settled.name: Financial Headroom Above Housing Charge

settled.decision: Kept as a distinct settled axis from financial_headroom. These axes have different directionality at the high end: financial_headroom treats higher income as strictly better (more buffer), while income_to_coop_range_fit has an optimal middle zone — income too low raises arrears risk, income too high reduces retention motivation. A very high-income household would score high on financial_headroom but potentially low on income_to_coop_range_fit. Report 0 surfaces this as a standalone concept and it is not re-carved in any other report.
settled.name: Income Fit to Co-op's Affordability Range

settled.decision: Kept as a distinct settled axis. It measures raw participation capacity (how many adults are available), which is different from available_time_for_coop_work (how many hours each adult has) and from income_earning_balance_coapplicant_availability (whether a low-earning co-applicant has daytime flexibility). A household with two fully employed adults scores high on headcount but differs from a single-adult household on that axis alone, independently of each person's schedule or income.
settled.name: Adult Contribution Headcount

settled.decision: Kept as a distinct settled axis from household_contribution_headcount and available_time_for_coop_work. This axis specifically captures a qualitative signal from income asymmetry — a low-earning co-applicant may have daytime schedule flexibility regardless of their willingness or total time budget. A household where both partners earn full-time equivalent incomes scores low here (both likely unavailable in daytime) even if both are highly willing and have good evening availability. Report 3 surfaces this as a standalone concept.
settled.name: Co-applicant Daytime Availability Signal

settled.decision: Kept as a distinct settled axis. A plausible applicant could score high here (runs a bicycle-restoration and upholstery repair café) while scoring low on trade_maintenance_skill (no licensed building trade) and low on community_programming_skill (does not organise social events or programming). The repair-café / reuse ethos is a specific contribution type not captured by any other axis. Report 1 surfaces this as a standalone concept.
settled.name: Repair-Café & Circular Economy Contribution

settled.decision: Kept as a distinct settled axis. Pet ownership is a factual household characteristic with direct implications for shared-space use and allergy exposure that no other axis captures. It is not reducible to values alignment, participation, or any skill dimension. Report 4 surfaces this as a standalone concept.
settled.name: Pet Ownership & Shared-Space Impact

settled.decision: Kept as a distinct settled axis. Household size is a factual characteristic that directly affects unit-allocation decisions and shared-space demand. It is not captured by any other axis — it is different from child age profile (which is about supervision demands) and from contribution headcount (which is about adult participation capacity). Report 3 surfaces this as a standalone concept.
settled.name: Household Size (Persons per Unit)

dimensions.definition: Experience serving in formal leadership or committee roles in a shared-governance body — co-op board, strata council, tenants' association executive, childcare co-op board — including managing agendas, running elections, keeping minutes, and navigating collective decision-making. Does not require the body to be a residential housing co-op.
dimensions.high_end: Served multiple terms on a formal board or council with demonstrated institutional knowledge: chairing meetings, conflict resolution, volunteer engagement, hand-off practices, and sustained stewardship of a collective body.
dimensions.low_end: No formal governance experience of any kind in any collective or community organisation; has not sat on any board, committee, or council.
dimensions.name: Formal Collective Governance Experience

dimensions.definition: The depth and directness of prior experience living in or holding a governance role within a housing co-op specifically — including first-hand knowledge of work hours, shared decision-making, consensus cycles, and co-op rhythms.
dimensions.high_end: Grew up in or lived in a housing co-op for multiple years, and/or served multiple terms in a formal co-op board or committee role, with demonstrated outcomes such as passed audits or documented processes handed to successors.
dimensions.low_end: No prior experience living in or governing any housing co-op; all co-op knowledge is second-hand, theoretical, or limited to analogous settings.
dimensions.name: Housing Co-op Governance & Living Experience

dimensions.definition: Depth of hands-on experience managing or auditing collective finances — bookkeeping, budget preparation, account reconciliation, audit readiness, and treasurer roles in a co-op or comparable shared-governance organisation.
dimensions.high_end: Has served as treasurer of a multi-unit co-op or organisation through multiple clean audits, prepared financial statements, reconciled accounts, and mentored a successor on financial records — or works professionally as a bookkeeper and has offered this skill to the co-op.
dimensions.low_end: No financial, bookkeeping, or collective budget experience of any kind; no offer of audit, accounts, or budget support made.
dimensions.name: Financial Governance & Bookkeeping Experience

dimensions.definition: The extent to which the household holds licensed or demonstrably practised trade qualifications — electrical, plumbing, mechanical, masonry, arboriculture, etc. — that cover common building-system repairs without hiring external contractors.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult holds a recognised trade licence or deeply practised skill (e.g. Red Seal plumber, licensed electrician, transit mechanic, tiler/mason) covering major building systems, and has offered to apply it on-site.
dimensions.low_end: Neither adult holds any relevant trade qualification; household explicitly acknowledges inability to perform hands-on building repairs and would need to pay contractors for all such work.
dimensions.name: Hands-On Building Trade Skill Depth

dimensions.definition: Experience managing the administrative side of building upkeep — scheduling tradespeople, soliciting and comparing quotes, tracking warranties, following repairs to completion, and documenting outcomes for future volunteers. Distinct from performing the physical work oneself.
dimensions.high_end: Has directly chaired or managed a maintenance committee or equivalent role, scheduling trades, obtaining multiple quotes, tracking repair warranties and work orders to completion over an extended period.
dimensions.low_end: No experience coordinating or procuring building maintenance work; any contribution is limited to direct personal labour or is entirely absent.
dimensions.name: Maintenance Coordination & Procurement Experience

dimensions.definition: Whether the household can contribute skilled labour to outdoor and grounds upkeep — arboriculture, horticulture, composting, food gardening, storm-damage clearance, landscaping — that would otherwise require hired specialists.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult is a certified arborist and/or experienced year-round gardener who explicitly offers to manage shared beds, compost, tree health, and grounds safety after weather events.
dimensions.low_end: No adult mentions any outdoor or grounds skills; contributions are limited to generic unskilled tasks or nothing outdoors is mentioned at all.
dimensions.name: Outdoor & Grounds Maintenance Skills

dimensions.definition: Whether the household can build or maintain digital tools that reduce administrative friction for the co-op — booking systems, maintenance-request platforms, newsletters, signage, financial spreadsheets, or communications design.
dimensions.high_end: Software developer or UX/graphic designer who explicitly offers to build and maintain a bespoke co-op management system, or provides design and spreadsheet infrastructure that the co-op would otherwise outsource.
dimensions.low_end: No digital, design, or systems-building skills described; no offer of any contribution to co-op digital or administrative infrastructure.
dimensions.name: Digital & Tech Infrastructure Skills

dimensions.definition: Whether the household holds formal professional or demonstrably practised experience mediating interpersonal disputes, facilitating difficult conversations, managing volunteer burnout, or applying HR-level conflict resolution in a shared-living or community context.
dimensions.high_end: Holds a professional background in HR mediation or dispute resolution, or has directly and demonstrably mediated member or neighbour disputes as a board or committee member, and has offered this capacity explicitly to the co-op.
dimensions.low_end: No described experience in professional mediation, conflict resolution, or HR-level people management; any conflict experience is as a party to a dispute rather than as a facilitator.
dimensions.name: Professional Conflict Mediation & HR Skills

dimensions.definition: Whether either adult holds a health or emergency-response professional background — nursing, paramedic, occupational health — that can be deployed for co-op member benefit through first-aid organisation, CPR instruction, or on-call health advice.
dimensions.high_end: Registered nurse or equivalent with explicit plans to establish a first-aid station, run CPR refreshers for members, or otherwise extend professional health skills to co-op benefit.
dimensions.low_end: No health or emergency-response professional background relevant to community benefit; no offer of health or safety infrastructure.
dimensions.name: Community Health & Safety Capacity

dimensions.definition: Whether the household brings skills that connect co-op members to the broader community or to public services — social work, community interpretation, newcomer navigation, or linking neighbours to city benefits and supports.
dimensions.high_end: Trained social worker or community interpreter who has explicitly offered to connect co-op members to city services, act as a language or cultural bridge, or navigate public benefits on behalf of neighbours — and has demonstrated this capacity through outside activities.
dimensions.low_end: No described skills or experience in community outreach, social services navigation, language bridging, or newcomer support; contributions are entirely inward-facing.
dimensions.name: Community Bridge & Social Services Navigation Skills

dimensions.definition: Whether the household can design and deliver programs for co-op members — children's activities, educational workshops, shared-meal coordination, sports coaching, language and cultural events — that build community life beyond maintenance obligations.
dimensions.high_end: Has professional skills directly applicable to resident programming (teacher offering homework clubs, cook offering shared-meal coordination, sports coach offering youth activities) and has specifically proposed running such programs as a co-op contribution.
dimensions.low_end: No educational, recreational, cooking, or community-programming skill or offer mentioned; contributions framed purely as physical maintenance or administrative tasks.
dimensions.name: Community Programming & Social Infrastructure Capacity

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the applicant explicitly commits — in their own words — to the full range of co-op participation obligations: work hours, committee roles, AGM attendance, and shared-governance duties, framed as a personal value rather than an obligation to be minimised.
dimensions.high_end: Explicitly volunteers for maintenance rotations, committee seats, AGMs, and work days; uses language like 'never miss a work day' or 'take on more than our share'; commitment is unconditional and proactively stated.
dimensions.low_end: Explicitly declines committee involvement or AGM attendance, states intention to do only the minimum, or signals plans to exit once a personal financial goal is met.
dimensions.name: Stated Commitment to Active Co-op Participation

dimensions.definition: How much discretionary time the household realistically has for co-op shifts and committee work after accounting for self-reported external commitments — employment demands, outside volunteer roles, touring schedules, board seats elsewhere, and caregiving obligations.
dimensions.high_end: Explicitly reports sparse outside commitments and abundant free time; no competing obligations disclosed; states willingness to take on more than the minimum share of co-op work hours.
dimensions.low_end: Self-reports a genuinely packed schedule (multiple outside boards, choir touring, repair café plus other boards) that would leave only minimum hours available for co-op duties.
dimensions.name: Realistic Available Time for Co-op Work

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the applicant's stated motivation for seeking co-op housing centres on cooperative values — mutual aid, shared responsibility, community-building — as ends in themselves, as opposed to primarily instrumental goals such as rent affordability or housing stability.
dimensions.high_end: Articulates a genuine co-operative philosophy lived independently of this application — mutual aid over individualism, democratic responsibility as meaningful in itself — demonstrated through past behaviour such as food sharing, tool libraries, or carpool networks.
dimensions.low_end: States that cost or stability is the primary or exclusive driver; co-op participation is framed as a necessary condition of the housing deal rather than a desired feature in itself ('mainly the rent is cheap'; 'we'd save the co-op money — that's it really').
dimensions.name: Intrinsic Co-op Philosophy Alignment

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the applicant demonstrates genuine, personal reflection on what cooperative housing means — going beyond slogans to show they have grappled with the tensions, trade-offs, and realities of shared governance.
dimensions.high_end: Describes specific lessons learned from collective living — about consensus, volunteer burnout, institutional fragility, or the quiet work behind a functioning community — in their own words, grounded in experience.
dimensions.low_end: Offers no reflection at all, or uses generic co-op language without any evidence of having actually grappled with the reality of cooperative governance.
dimensions.name: Depth of Reflection on Co-op Living

dimensions.definition: The degree to which an applicant demonstrates comfort and patience working within collective decision-making processes — accepting majority outcomes, engaging respectfully with disagreement, and supporting the group's process even when it moves slowly.
dimensions.high_end: Explicitly describes learning to disagree in a meeting and leave as friends, valuing the patience that consensus requires, or framing the democratic process of co-op governance as a feature rather than a friction.
dimensions.low_end: Describes impatience with group processes, a tendency to take over when others seem disorganised, or explicit refusal to participate in meetings and collective decisions.
dimensions.name: Collaborative Disposition in Shared Governance

dimensions.definition: Whether the applicant's disclosed history of disagreements in collective or governance settings reflects constructive, collaborative navigation — and the quality of reflection shown — versus adversarial escalation or a pattern of unresolved disputes.
dimensions.high_end: No disclosed conflicts with collective governance bodies, or has navigated disagreements constructively leaving relationships intact; explicitly values mediation and early intervention; demonstrates clear reflection and growth from past difficulties.
dimensions.low_end: Discloses clashes with multiple co-op or strata boards, a tendency to take over when frustrated, or a dispute that escalated to small-claims court — without a convincing account of learning or changed behaviour.
dimensions.name: Constructive Conflict Disposition & History

dimensions.definition: Whether the applicant proactively discloses genuine limitations, scheduling conflicts, skill gaps, or past difficulties rather than presenting only a curated positive picture — enabling the committee to make an informed assessment.
dimensions.high_end: Openly names a real constraint or limitation without being asked (thin availability due to touring, minimum hours due to outside boards, a prior conflict history, explicit skill gaps), demonstrating honesty and realistic self-knowledge.
dimensions.low_end: Presents only an idealised self-portrait with no disclosed limitations, constraints, or past difficulties — making it impossible to distinguish genuine enthusiasm from overselling.
dimensions.name: Candour About Constraints & Limitations

dimensions.definition: Whether the household already has direct personal ties to current members of this specific co-op, giving them firsthand familiarity with its culture, expectations, and day-to-day operations before applying.
dimensions.high_end: Has close friends or family who are current members, has visited or spent substantial time at the co-op through those relationships, and is already familiar with its specific culture, rhythms, and community norms firsthand.
dimensions.low_end: No known existing connection to any current member; the co-op is entirely unfamiliar at a personal level; knowledge comes from the application process alone.
dimensions.name: Existing Personal Connection to This Co-op

dimensions.definition: The range and regularity of the household's voluntary contributions to community life outside the co-op — coaching, charity events, repair cafés, arts groups, free classes, community interpretation — as evidence of a sustained community-oriented disposition.
dimensions.high_end: Actively and regularly engaged in multiple forms of outside community volunteering (e.g. youth sports coaching, charity race marshalling, repair café participation, community orchestra fundraising, teaching free classes) demonstrating an ingrained habit of civic contribution.
dimensions.low_end: Little or no evidence of voluntary community involvement outside the home; household presents only private hobbies or family life with no outward-facing volunteering.
dimensions.name: Broader Volunteer & Civic Engagement

dimensions.definition: Whether the ages of the household's children indicate that the household is likely to remain at its current size and composition for many years, providing stable long-term occupancy and engagement, versus facing near-term household shrinkage as children leave home.
dimensions.high_end: Children are young (toddlers to early school age), meaning the household will remain at its current size for a decade or more — providing stable, long-term occupancy and sustained family engagement with the co-op.
dimensions.low_end: All or most children are teenagers, meaning the household will shrink substantially within a few years as children leave home, creating uncertainty about long-term fit for the unit size.
dimensions.name: Children's Age — Household Stability Horizon

dimensions.definition: Whether the household signals an intention to remain in the co-op as a stable, long-term home rather than treating membership as a transitional stepping stone toward another housing goal.
dimensions.high_end: Frames the co-op explicitly as a long-term or permanent home; uses language about ageing in place, raising children here over many years, or investing in the community for the long run.
dimensions.low_end: Explicitly states plans to leave once saving enough for a down payment, or otherwise frames co-op membership as a temporary cost-saving arrangement.
dimensions.name: Long-Term Residency Intent

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the applicant frames their contribution in terms of documenting processes, mentoring successors, building institutional memory, and onboarding new members — ensuring the co-op's operational knowledge survives member turnover.
dimensions.high_end: Has explicitly mentored a successor (e.g. next treasurer), written processes so the next volunteer is not lost, kept systems documented as a stated priority, or explicitly offers to run new-member orientation and onboarding.
dimensions.low_end: Contribution framed entirely in terms of personal task completion; no mention of knowledge-sharing, documentation, continuity planning, or support for incoming members.
dimensions.name: Knowledge Transfer, Documentation & Onboarding Orientation

dimensions.definition: Experience designing or directly running new-member orientation programs, managing work-shift schedules, and helping newcomers integrate into a co-operative community — as distinct from managing ongoing volunteer retention.
dimensions.high_end: Has directly run new-member orientation programs, managed work-shift rosters for a co-op or association, and explicitly volunteers to take on this role, making new members feel welcomed and engaged from day one.
dimensions.low_end: No experience with member onboarding, orientation, or work-shift coordination; no offer to take on this function.
dimensions.name: Member Onboarding & Orientation Skill

dimensions.definition: The length of continuous employment for both adults in their current roles, jointly considered, as a proxy for income reliability and likelihood of sustained housing-charge payment capacity.
dimensions.high_end: Both adults have been continuously employed in their current roles for several years (five or more), suggesting stable, tested income streams with low near-term disruption risk.
dimensions.low_end: One or both adults started their current job very recently (within the past year or two), representing limited track record and higher uncertainty about income continuity.
dimensions.name: Employment Tenure Stability

dimensions.definition: How evenly household income is distributed between the two adults, measuring the household's resilience if one earner's income is interrupted through job loss, illness, parental leave, or relationship change.
dimensions.high_end: Income is split near-equally between both adults (each contributing roughly 40–60% of total), so the household can continue meeting obligations even if either income is disrupted.
dimensions.low_end: Income is heavily concentrated in one earner (one adult contributing 80%+ of total household income), making the household highly vulnerable to that earner's income being interrupted.
dimensions.name: Household Income Balance (Single-Earner Resilience)

dimensions.definition: The total annual household income relative to the applicant pool, indicating the financial cushion available to meet housing charges reliably and absorb unexpected levies or temporary income disruption without default risk.
dimensions.high_end: Household income well above the pool median, providing a substantial buffer against housing charge increases, special levies, or periods of reduced income.
dimensions.low_end: Household income at the lower end of the pool, leaving limited margin to absorb unexpected costs or housing charge adjustments.
dimensions.name: Household Income Financial Headroom

dimensions.definition: How well the household's income aligns with the co-op's typical member income range — sufficient to reliably meet housing charges without being so far above the range that co-op pricing no longer offers meaningful benefit and long-term retention becomes uncertain.
dimensions.high_end: Household income sits comfortably within the range where co-op housing is genuinely affordable and meaningful, making reliable payment likely without over-qualification.
dimensions.low_end: Household income is either very low (raising payment-reliability concerns) or substantially above the range where co-op pricing is the primary draw, potentially reducing long-term retention motivation.
dimensions.name: Income Fit to Co-op's Affordability Range

dimensions.definition: The number of adults in the household whose time and skills are available for co-op obligations, reflecting the household's raw participation capacity.
dimensions.high_end: Two adults, both able and willing to contribute to work shifts and committee roles, effectively doubling the household's participation capacity.
dimensions.low_end: Practically only one adult is available for co-op contributions — whether because the co-applicant is absent, unavailable, or the applicant signals only one will be active.
dimensions.name: Adult Contribution Headcount

dimensions.definition: Whether the co-applicant's modest or part-time income suggests greater daytime flexibility for co-op tasks that require a household member to be present during working hours — trades access, deliveries, and building emergencies.
dimensions.high_end: Co-applicant earns a modest income suggestive of part-time or flexible hours, meaning the household likely has daytime coverage for building-access tasks that full-time workers cannot handle.
dimensions.low_end: Both adults earn similar full-time incomes, suggesting both are likely unavailable during standard working hours for daytime co-op coordination tasks.
dimensions.name: Co-applicant Daytime Availability Signal

dimensions.definition: Whether the household brings hands-on repair and reuse skills — sewing and upholstery, bicycle restoration, small-engine servicing — that support a culture of fixing rather than replacing shared assets.
dimensions.high_end: Already runs or volunteers at repair cafés, restores and donates bicycles, or has directly applicable repair/reuse skills (upholstery, small-engine mechanics) and explicitly proposes to operate repair sessions within the co-op.
dimensions.low_end: No repair-culture skill or engagement; no evidence of involvement in reuse, restoration, or fix-it activities of any kind.
dimensions.name: Repair-Café & Circular Economy Contribution

dimensions.definition: The number and type of pets in the household, relevant to shared-space rules, allergen considerations, noise, and neighbour relations in a dense co-op setting.
dimensions.high_end: No pets, or a single small low-allergen pet, minimising shared-space friction and allergen risk for neighbours.
dimensions.low_end: Multiple pets (e.g. dog and cat, multiple dogs, or rotating foster animals), or an undecided pet situation, creating higher shared-space impact and uncertainty for the co-op.
dimensions.name: Pet-Related Shared-Space Friction Risk

dimensions.definition: The total number of people in the household (adults plus children), relevant to unit occupancy, space needs, and — in larger households — the potential for multiple adults to contribute to co-op work.
dimensions.high_end: Smaller household with fewer occupants, implying lower space demand per unit and simpler occupancy logistics.
dimensions.low_end: Larger household with four or more children, implying greater space requirements and higher occupancy density.
dimensions.name: Household Size

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the applicant describes valuing and actively nurturing relationships with neighbours and co-residents — through hospitality, care, and genuine community-building — beyond fulfilling formal obligations.
dimensions.high_end: Describes specific practices of neighbourly warmth: hosting potlucks, baking for neighbours, looking out for elderly residents, or explicitly framing the co-op as a place to form meaningful lasting relationships with people they live beside.
dimensions.low_end: Describes a preference for keeping to themselves; emphasises privacy or transactional participation; no relational or hospitality dimension described.
dimensions.name: Interpersonal Warmth & Community-Building Orientation

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the application essays are grounded in a genuine personal voice, concrete verifiable experiences, named organisations, and specific past outcomes — as opposed to generic language, corporate jargon, or terse non-answers that leave the committee unable to assess fit.
dimensions.high_end: Essays are written in a clear, honest personal voice with specific details, named past roles, concrete outcomes, and evidence that allows the committee to verify claims and form a clear picture of the household's values and likely behaviour.
dimensions.low_end: Essays consist of impersonal corporate jargon, generic buzzwords, a single dismissive sentence, or extreme self-deprecation — providing no concrete basis for assessment and leaving the committee unable to evaluate the household.
dimensions.name: Essay Specificity, Authenticity & Concrete Evidence
